nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on tropical Western Atlantic coral reefs and reef-rubble zones, from intertidal shallows down to roughly 80 m. Tends to scavenge over hard substrate, ledges, and live rock, occupying small gastropod shells (commonly cerith, nassarius, and similar shapes).

decorPreferences: live rock with crevices and ledges
open scavenging surfaces for algae grazing
ample supply of empty gastropod shells of varying sizes

diet.notes: Generally considered a hardy omnivorous scavenger and grazer. Has been documented working through nuisance filamentous, hair, and slime algae as well as cyanobacteria, and picking continuously at detritus, uneaten food, and decomposing material. In a well-established reef tank tends to feed itself on biofilm and leftovers; if the tank is clean enough that natural food runs short, supplement with dried marine algae (nori), sinking pellets, finely chopped frozen meaty foods, or invertebrate diets. Typically not picky about prepared foods.

goodWith: most LPS, SPS, and soft corals
conspecifics (typically tolerated when shells and food are abundant)
avoidWith: predatory fish that target inverts (lionfish, large wrasses, large dottybacks, hawkfish, triggerfish)
large aggressive crabs (Mithrax/emerald, larger pseudo-Calappa, etc.)
any copper-based medication in the system
tanks short on empty shells — competition can drive shell-fighting and occasional snail predation

summary: The Scarlet Reef Hermit Crab (Paguristes cadenati) is a small, bright-red Caribbean species widely kept in reef aquariums as part of the cleanup crew. It is generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly, with an appetite for hair algae, cyanobacteria, and detritus.
careNotes:
The Scarlet Reef Hermit Crab is among the most commonly kept Caribbean cleanup-crew hermits, often found alongside the smaller blue-legged Clibanarius tricolor. It is recognizable by its bright scarlet legs and chelipeds, yellow to yellowish-orange eyestalks, and smooth, largely hairless pincers. Adults are typically 2.5–5 cm across including the shell. It scavenges almost continuously over live rock, substrate, and ledges and is commonly kept to control nuisance algae — hair, slime, and cyanobacteria — and to consume leftover meaty food.

It is generally considered slightly larger and modestly more assertive than C. tricolor, but still on the peaceful end of the spectrum. Like other hermit crabs, it MUST be provided with an ample supply of empty gastropod shells in a range of sizes — when shells run short, hermits are well documented attacking snails (Astrea, Trochus, Nerite) to evict them from their shells. Stocking spare shells, and choosing shapes the species favors such as cerith-like forms, is one of the most effective steps for keeping it peaceful in a mixed cleanup crew.

Like all decapod crustaceans, P. cadenati is highly intolerant of copper-based medications and of sharp swings in salinity, temperature, or pH. Drip-acclimate over 1–2 hours on arrival. Iodine supplementation is sometimes recommended to support clean molts, but stable reef-tank parameters and a varied diet are typically sufficient. Newly molted individuals are vulnerable for a day or two while the new exoskeleton hardens and tend to hide; leave the shed exuvia in the tank for the crab to consume.

Stocking density is commonly given as roughly 1 hermit per 4–8 L of tank water, though this depends on algae load and feeding regime. Overstocked tanks tend to see more shell competition and occasional cyanobacteria or algae rebounds once the available food is consumed.

This species is sometimes sold in the trade as "Mexican Red Leg Hermit", but the true Mexican Red Leg is Clibanarius digueti (or in some sources C. californiensis) — a distinct species from the Eastern Pacific. P. cadenati is the Caribbean and Western Atlantic species.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Paguristes cadenati is not routinely reported in the hobby. As with most marine hermits, larvae are planktonic and would require a dedicated larval-rearing system with appropriate live feeds to raise; nearly all aquarium stock is wild-collected from the Caribbean.

additional.name: ReefApp — Paguristes cadenati

Higher tank-size figure was noted but the 40 L cleanup-crew floor was retained.

additional.name: Aquarium Specialty — The Friendly and Reef Safe Scarlet Reef Hermit Crab
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~1.5 in), temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, peaceful temperament, reef-safe status, lifespan 2–4 years, omnivorous scavenger diet, shell-supply requirement for molting, and the trade synonyms 'Red Reef' and 'Red Legged Hermit Crab'.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally molts roughly every 3–6 weeks under stable reef conditions; younger and faster-growing individuals molt more often. After molting, the crab is soft and vulnerable for 24–48 hours and typically hides — leave the shed exuvia for it to eat (recovers calcium and trace elements). Adequate iodine and stable salinity are commonly cited as supporting clean molts. Because the crab also outgrows its shell over time, keep a varied supply of empty gastropod shells in the tank so it can move up in size without attacking live snails for theirs.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Not routinely captive-bred in the aquarium hobby. Eggs hatch as planktonic zoea larvae; rearing would require a dedicated larval system with appropriate live feeds. Hobby stock is essentially all wild-collected from the Caribbean.
